title: Quassia sanguinea
intro: Quassia sanguinea, also known as the Blood Quassia or Quassia amara, is a plant species belonging to the family Rutaceae, commonly used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties. The health benefits of Quassia sanguinea include its antiparasitic, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help treat various health conditions such as intestinal worms, skin infections, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of this plant include quassin, which is a lignan that exhibits potent antiparasitic and antibacterial activities, as well as other compounds like quassinoid and amarinin. Quassia sanguinea is often used to prepare herbal remedies such as tinctures, infusions, and decoctions, which are used to treat various health conditions and promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Quassia sanguinea is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Simaroubaceae. It is native to tropical regions of South America and is also known as the "blood quassia" due to its dark-colored sap. The plant is part of the genus Quassia, which includes several other species of flowering plants. The Simaroubaceae family is a group of flowering plants that are commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions around the world. This family is known for its unique and diverse range of plant species, many of which have medicinal and aromatic properties.
uses: Quassia sanguinea is known to have been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant is said to be used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its bark and roots are often used in folk medicine to create remedies for these issues. The plant's extracts are also believed to have antiseptic and antifungal properties, making it useful for wound care. However, more research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Quassia sanguinea.

side_effects: Quassia sanguinea has been found to cause gastrointestinal issues and allergic reactions in some individuals due to its toxic properties. The plant's volatile oils can irritate the skin, eyes, and mucous membranes, leading to discomfort. Consuming large amounts of Quassia sanguinea can cause vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.
